perturbation.original_text: 1.6 Distributor's Terms and Minimum Expectations. In order to maintain the exclusive rights to sell, lease, distribute and service Products in the Market, the Distributor must use all commercially reasonably efforts to purchase for sale to subdistributors the following minimum quantities of the Products from the Company: On the commencement of the Term Distributor will issue to the Company an irrevocable letter of credit ("LC") in the amount of Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($500,000), the form of which is attached hereto as Exhibit A and incorporated herein by reference. The LC shall have a two (2) month term, and shall be renewed for five (5) consecutive bimonthly periods. A minimum of a $250,000.00 purchase order must be received by Company by the first of each month for a total (12) month period.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 Distributor's Terms and Minimum Expectations. In order to maintain the exclusive rights to sell, lease, distribute and service Products in the Market, the Distributor must purchase for sale to subdistributors the following minimum quantities of the Products from the Company: On the commencement of the Term Distributor will issue to the Company an irrevocable letter of credit ("LC") in the amount of Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($500,000), the form of which is attached hereto as Exhibit A and incorporated herein by reference. The LC shall have a two (2) month term, and shall be renewed for five (5) consecutive bimonthly periods. A minimum of a $250,000.00 purchase order must be received by Company by the first of each month for a total (12) month period.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "use all commercially reasonably efforts" the term becomes now strictly binding. This contradicts with section 2.2 where Company's Obligation can be omitted because of external factors and also the term "commercially reasonably efforts" used in section 1.6 shows a non-strict liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 Shipment. The Company and the Distributor shall jointly determine shipment dates. The Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to ship promptly all orders for Products received from the Distributor. In addition to any other remedy which this Agreement provides to Distributor against Company, if Company fails to deliver or delays in delivering Products as were ordered by Distributor within 45 days after their required delivery date, and if as a result of such failure or delay Distributor loses its customer's orders for those Products, the number of units which Distributor ordered but were not timely delivered to Distributor or to Distributor's customer will be credited against Distributor's minimum expectation as specified on Section 1.6 of this Agreement. The Company may refuse to accept a purchase order on the grounds that it cannot meet the delivery schedule therein, and if as a result of such failure or delay Distributor loses its customer's orders for those Products, the number of units which Distributor ordered but were not timely delivered to Distributor or to Distributor's customer will be credited against Distributor's minimum. Distributor shall make reasonable efforts to notify the Company of the proposed delivery schedule before accepting a customer order and shall give the Company written notice of any customer purchase orders which imposes liability for late shipment and neither the Distributor nor the Company shall have a liability for consequential or liquidated damages pertaining to late delivery unless Company specifically acknowledges and agrees in writing to the same. The Distributor agrees that the Company shall not be liable for its failure to perform due to any occurrence beyond the Company's reasonable control, including, but not limited to, acts of God, fires, Page -4- floods, wars, sabotage, accidents in shipping beyond the Company's control, labor strikes other than strikes against the Company itself, weather conditions or foreign or domestic government regulation or authority which directly affects Company's ability to deliver Product.
perturbation.justification: The original text included "use all commercially reasonably efforts," creating a flexibility for the distributor. By removing this phrase, the obligation to purchase minimum quantities becomes strictly binding. This directly contradicts section 2.2, which explicitly allows for exceptions to the Company's shipping obligations due to external factors and references 'commercially reasonable efforts', thus impacting the distributor's obligation to purchase minimum quantities as detailed in Section 1.6. The removal of the flexibility in 1.6 directly conflicts with the allowable exceptions in 2.2, where a failure to deliver on the company's part is also a credit for minimum expectations. This creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.7 Payment Terms. Distributor shall pay Company within thirty (30) days of Distributor's or, as the case may be, the end-user's receipt of Products.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.7 Payment Terms. Distributor shall pay Company within sixty (60) days of Distributor's or, as the case may be, the end-user's receipt of Products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms from 30 to 60 days creates a contradiction with section 4.2 (A), where failure to make prompt payment is a cause for termination. "Full and prompt payment" now becomes ambiguous since it's unclear if 'prompt' refers to the original 30 days or the new 60 days, or what 'prompt' means for any amounts due before the modification.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Termination for Cause. Either party may terminate this Agreement upon 30 days Page -8- prior written notice to the other upon the occurrence of any of the following events: (A) the Distributor's failure to make full and prompt payment to the Company of all sums due and owing to it; (b) either party's default in the performance of any of the material, terms, conditions, obligations, undertakings, covenants or liabilities set forth herein and such default is not cured within a commercially reasonable time after the defaulting party has been notified of the default by the other party and (c) as otherwise expressly provided herein. In the event either party (a) becomes adjudicated insolvent, (b) discontinues its business, (c) has voluntary of involuntary bankruptcy proceedings instituted against it, or (d) makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors, the other party shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement effective immediately upon written notice.
perturbation.justification: The change from '30 days' to '60 days' in the payment terms directly contradicts section 4.2(A), which allows termination for failure to make 'full and prompt payment.' The word 'prompt' is now ambiguous, as the original 30-day period is no longer explicitly enforced, creating a direct conflict regarding payment obligations and termination rights. It creates uncertainty if prompt refers to the original 30 days or the new 60 days, and this is a critical element for contract termination.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 Product Liability Insurance. Company will carry a reasonable amount of product liability insurance through a reasonably acceptable products liability insurance company and will name the Distributor as an additional insured under that policy. Company will make reasonable efforts to procure a policy, which is non-cancelable, except upon thirty (30) days, advance notice to the Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 Product Liability Insurance. Company will obtain product liability insurance. Company will make reasonable efforts to procure a policy, which is non-cancelable, except upon thirty (30) days, advance notice to the Distributor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing “Company will carry a reasonable amount of product liability insurance through a reasonably acceptable products liability insurance company and will name the Distributor as an additional insured under that policy” creates a contradiction, as the company is no longer required to include the distributor under product liability insurance, contradicting 5.3 which states “Company and Distributor agree to indemnify, defend and hold each other harmless from any and all suits, claims, obligations, liabilities, damages, losses and the like”
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3 Indemnification. Company and Distributor agree to indemnify, defend and hold each other harmless from any and all suits, claims, obligations, liabilities, damages, losses and the like (including attorneys' fees and costs) relating to or arising out of: (A) any breach of any material representations, warranties, covenants, obligations, agreements or duties in connection with this Agreement; (b) any negligence or fault; (c) any violation by either of them of the patent, copyright, trademark or other Page -10- intellectual property rights of third parties. In addition, Company agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Distributor from and against all suits, claims, obligations, liabilities, damages, losses and the like (including attorneys' fees and costs) arising out of or related to Company's manufacture or design of the Products, provided that Distributor is not at fault in connection with the same, and Distributor agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Company from and against all suits, claims, obligations, liabilities, damages, losses and the like (including a attorneys' fees and costs) arising out of or related to Distributor's sales, marketing practices or unauthorized Product alteration (provided that Company is not at fault in connection with same).
perturbation.justification: The original text required the company to name the distributor as an additional insured under its product liability insurance policy. The changed text removes this requirement. Section 5.3 states that 'Company and Distributor agree to indemnify, defend and hold each other harmless from any and all suits, claims, obligations, liabilities, damages, losses and the like'. If the distributor is not named as an additional insured, the company may not adequately protect the distributor from product liability claims, potentially contradicting the indemnification agreement outlined in 5.3. This creates a strong contradiction because the distributor is no longer automatically covered under the company's insurance policy, potentially leading to financial risk that the original agreement intended to mitigate. The Perturbation Type 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' also matches, because key obligations are no longer consistent.
